sections.section_title: Short title
sections.content: This Act (hereinafter referred to as 'the Code') may be cited as the Code of Criminal Procedure Act.

sections.section_title: Interpretation
sections.content: Defines key terms used within the Code, including but not limited to: 'appointed date', 'Attorney-General', 'Bailable offence', 'Non-bailable offence', 'Child abuse', 'Cognizable offence', 'Complaint', 'Corporation', 'Court of Appeal', 'Discharge', 'Enactment', 'Explosives', 'Fine', 'Fiscal', 'Government Agent', 'Government Analyst', 'Government Examiner of Questioned Documents', 'Government medical officer', 'High Court', 'Indictable offence', 'Information Book', 'Inquirer', 'Inquiry', 'Island', 'Judge', 'Judicial proceeding', 'Magistrate', 'Non-cognizable offence', 'Offence', 'Offensive weapon', 'Officer in charge of a police station', 'Peace officer', 'Penal Code', 'Person', 'Place of safety', 'Pleader', 'Police officer', 'Police station', 'Prescribed', 'Registrar', 'Registrar of Finger Prints', 'Solicitor-General', 'State Counsel', 'Summary offence', 'Supreme Court', 'Suspended sentence', 'Writing'.

sections.section_title: Words referring to acts include illegal omissions. Words to have same meaning as in the Penal Code. Signatures to be in handwriting.
sections.content: (1) Words which refer to acts done extend to illegal omissions. (2) All words and expressions used herein and defined in the Penal Code and not hereinbefore defined shall be deemed to have the meanings respectively attributed to them by that Code. (3) Whenever by or for the purposes of this Code any person is directed or required to sign a document the signature must be written with a pen or other like instrument and must not be affixed or impressed by a stamp or other like means.

sections.section_title: Powers, jurisdiction rights and duties of certain officers.
sections.content: Where a power, jurisdiction or right is conferred, or a duty is imposed, on the Attorney-General, Solicitor-General, Inspector-General of Police, Government Analyst, Government Examiner of Questioned Documents, Registrar of Finger Prints, or the Registrar of a court, then, such power, jurisdiction or right may be exercised and the duty performed by the holder for the time being of the office or the acting holder of such office or the additional or the deputy or the assistant officer. Such additional, deputy or assistant officer shall include any person appointed to act in such office.

sections.section_title: Trial of offences under Penal Code and other laws.
sections.content: All offences - (a) under the Penal Code, (b) under any other law unless otherwise specially provided for in that law or any other law, shall be inquired into, tried, and otherwise dealt with according to the provisions of this Code.

sections.section_title: Saving of powers of Supreme Court, Court of Appeal and Attorney-General.
sections.content: Anything in this Code shall not be construed as derogating from or limiting the powers or jurisdiction of the Supreme Court or of the Court of Appeal or of the Judges thereof or of the Attorney-General.

sections.section_title: Cases not provided for.
sections.content: As regards matters of criminal procedure for which special provisions may not have been made by this Code or by any other law for the time being in force such procedure as the justice of the case may require and as is not inconsistent with this Code may be followed.

sections.section_title: Power of Supreme Court and Court of Appeal to inspect courts of first instance.
sections.content: In addition to other powers vested in the Supreme Court and the Court of Appeal, such courts in the exercise of their powers of superintendence may inspect all courts of first instance and give directions as to the keeping of the records thereof.

sections.section_title: Criminal summary jurisdiction of Magistrates' Courts.
sections.content: Subject to and in accordance with the provisions of this Code every Magistrate's Court shall have - (a) power and authority and is hereby required to hear, try, determine, and dispose of in a summary way all suits or prosecutions for offences committed wholly or in part within its local jurisdiction... (b) Jurisdiction (i) to inquire into all offences committed or alleged to have been committed wholly or in part within its local jurisdiction... (ii) to issue warrants to search... and to require persons to furnish security for the peace or for their good behaviour... (iii) to inquire into all cases in which any person shall die in any prison or mental or leprosy hospital or shall come to his death by violence or accident, or when death shall have occurred suddenly, or when the body of any person shall be found dead without its being known how such person came by his death.

sections.section_title: Offences under Penal code
sections.content: Subject to the other provisions of this Code any offence under the Penal Code whether committed before or after the appointed date may be tried save as otherwise specially provided for in any law (a) by the High Court ; or (b) by a Magistrate's Court where that offence is shown in the eighth column of the First Schedule to be triable by a Magistrate's Court.

sections.section_title: Offences under other laws.
sections.content: Any offence under any law other than the Penal Code whether committed before or after the appointed date shall be tried save as otherwise specially provided for in any law - (a) where a court is mentioned in that behalf in that law... (b) where a court is not mentioned in that behalf in that law...

sections.section_title: High Court to try cases only upon indictment.
sections.content: Subject to the provisions of this Code and of any other written law the High Court shall not take cognizance of any offence unless the accused person has been indicted before it for trial by or at the instance of the Attorney-General.

sections.section_title: Sentence which High Court may impose.
sections.content: The High Court may impose any sentence or other penalty prescribed by written law.

sections.section_title: Sentence which a Magistrate's Court may impose.
sections.content: A Magistrate's Court may impose any of the following sentences: (a) imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding two years; (b) fine not exceeding one thousand five hundred rupees; (d) any lawful sentence combining any of the sentences aforesaid: Provided that anything in this section shall not be deemed to repeal the provisions of any enactment in force whereby special powers of punishment are given.

sections.section_title: No sentence of imprisonment for a term of less than seven days. Sentence of detention in precincts of court in lieu of imprisonment. Power of courts to sentence to imprisonment in default of payment of fine.
sections.content: (1) Notwithstanding anything in this Code, the Penal Code, or any other written law to the contrary, any court shall not sentence any person to imprisonment, whether in default of payment of a fine or not, for a term which is less than seven days. (2) Any court may... in lieu of imposing a sentence of imprisonment... order that the offender be detained in the precincts of the court until such hour on the day on which the order is made, not being later than 8 p.m.... (3) Any court may impose such term of imprisonment in default of payment of a fine as is authorized by law... (4) The imprisonment imposed under subsection (3) may be in addition to a substantive sentence of imprisonment...

sections.section_title: Sentence in case of conviction for several offences at one trial.
sections.content: (1) When a person is convicted at one trial of two or more distinct offences the court may, subject to section 301, sentence him for such offences to the several punishments prescribed therefor... such punishments when consisting of imprisonment to commence... the one after the expiration of the other... Provided that if the case is tried by a Magistrate's Court the aggregate punishment shall not exceed twice the amount of punishment which such court in the exercise of its ordinary jurisdiction is competent to inflict. (2) For the purpose of appeal aggregate sentences imposed under subsection (1)... shall be deemed to be a single sentence.

sections.section_title: Payment of costs compensation and upon acquittal.
sections.content: Details provisions for ordering a complainant to pay State costs and compensation to the accused if the complaint is found frivolous or vexatious; compensation for arrest without sufficient grounds; and compensation to victims upon conviction. Also covers recovery of sums and application of fines.

sections.section_title: Community Service Orders.
sections.content: Allows a court to order a convicted person to perform community service in lieu of imprisonment or in default of fine payment. Specifies the nature of service, duration (40-240 hours within one year), consequences of non-compliance, and the role of the authorized officer.

chapters.chapter_title: OF AID AND INFORMATION TO THE MAGISTRATES AND POLICE AND PERSONS MAKING ARRESTS

sections.section_title: Public when to assist Magistrates and peace officers.
sections.content: Every person is bound to assist a Magistrate or a peace officer reasonably demanding his aid - (a) in the taking of any other person whom such Magistrate or peace officer is authorized to arrest; (b) in the prevention of a breach of the peace or of any injury attempted to be committed to any public property; (c) in the suppression of a riot or any affray.

sections.section_title: Aid to other than peace officers executing warrant.
sections.content: When a warrant is directed to a person other than a peace officer any other person may aid in the execution of such warrant if the person to whom the warrant is directed be near at hand and acting in the execution of his warrant.

sections.section_title: Public to give information about certain offences.
sections.content: Every person aware of (a) the commission of or the intention of any other person to commit certain specified offences under the Penal Code; (b) of any sudden or unnatural death or death by violence, or of any death under suspicious circumstances... shall... forthwith give information to the nearest Magistrate's Court or to the officer in charge of the nearest police station...

sections.section_title: Peace officer bound to report certain matters.
sections.content: Every peace officer shall forthwith communicate to the nearest Magistrate or inquirer... any information which he may have or obtain respecting - (a) the commission of or the attempt to commit any offence within the local jurisdiction... (b) the occurrence therein of any sudden or unnatural death or of any death by violence or under suspicious circumstances; (c) the finding of the dead body of any person without it being known how such person came by death.

subchapters.subchapter_title: A. - ARREST GENERALLY

sections.section_title: Arrest how made.
sections.content: (1) In making an arrest the person making the same shall actually touch or confine the body of the person to be arrested unless there be a submission to the custody by word or action and shall inform the person to be arrested of the nature of the charge or allegation upon which he is arrested. Explanation: Keeping a person in confinement... shall be deemed to be an arrest... (2) If such person forcibly resists... the person making the arrest may use such means as are reasonably necessary to effect the arrest. (3) ...shall not give a right to cause the death of a person who is not accused of an offence punishable with death.

sections.section_title: Search of place entered by person sought to be arrested.
sections.content: If any person acting under a warrant of arrest or having authority to arrest has reason to believe that any person to be arrested has entered into or is within any place, the person residing in or in charge of such place shall on demand... allow him free ingress therein and afford all reasonable facilities for a search therein.

sections.section_title: Procedure where ingress not obtainable.
sections.content: If ingress to such place cannot be obtained under section 24 it shall be lawful... for a peace officer to enter such place and search therein, and in order to effect an entrance into such place to break open any outer or inner door or window... if after notification of his authority and purpose and demand of admittance duly made he cannot otherwise obtain admittance.

sections.section_title: Search of person in place searched under warrant.
sections.content: Whenever a search for anything is or is about to be lawfully made in any house or place... all persons found therein may be lawfully detained... until the search is completed, and... they may... be searched...

sections.section_title: Power to break open doors and windows for purposes of liberation.
sections.content: Any person authorized to make an arrest may break open any outer or inner door or window of any place in order to liberate himself or any other person who, having lawfully entered for the purpose of making an arrest, is detained therein.

sections.section_title: No unnecessary restraint.
sections.content: The person arrested shall not be subjected to more restraint than is necessary to prevent his escape.

sections.section_title: Search of persons arrested.
sections.content: Specifies when a peace officer may search a person who has been arrested and place articles found upon them in safe custody.

sections.section_title: Mode of searching women.
sections.content: Whenever it is necessary to cause a woman to be searched the search shall be made by another woman with strict regard to decency.

sections.section_title: Power to seize offensive weapons.
sections.content: The person making any arrest under this Code may take from the person arrested any offensive weapons... and shall deliver all weapons and instruments so taken to the court or officer before which or whom the person making the arrest is required by law to produce the person arrested.

subchapters.subchapter_title: B. - ARREST WITHOUT A WARRANT

sections.section_title: When peace officers may arrest without warrant
sections.content: Lists the circumstances under which a peace officer may arrest without a warrant, including: commission of a breach of peace in their presence; reasonable suspicion of involvement in a cognizable offence; possession of house-breaking implements; being a proclaimed offender; possession of suspected stolen property; obstruction of a peace officer; being a suspected deserter; taking precautions to conceal presence with a view to committing an offence; and for extradition-related matters.

sections.section_title: Powers of arrest in non-cognizable cases.
sections.content: Allows arrest in non-cognizable cases if the accused refuses to give name and residence or gives false details, or if they have no permanent residence and are about to leave Sri Lanka.

sections.section_title: Pursuit of offenders into other jurisdiction.
sections.content: For the purpose of arresting any person whom he has power to arrest without a warrant a peace officer may pursue any such person into any part of Sri Lanka.

sections.section_title: Arrest by private persons, procedure on such arrest.
sections.content: Any private person may arrest any person who in his presence commits a cognizable offence or who has been proclaimed as an offender, or who is running away and whom he reasonably suspects of having committed a cognizable offence, and shall without unnecessary delay make over the person so arrested to the nearest peace officer...

sections.section_title: How person arrested is to be dealt with.
sections.content: A peace officer making an arrest without warrant shall without unnecessary delay and subject to the provisions herein contained as to bail take or send the person arrested before a Magistrate having jurisdiction in the case.

sections.section_title: Person arrested not to be detained more than twenty-four hours.
sections.content: Any peace officer shall not detain in custody... a person arrested without a warrant for a longer period than... twenty-four hours exclusive of the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the Magistrate.

sections.section_title: Police to report arrests.
sections.content: Officers in charge of police stations shall report to the Magistrates' Courts of their respective districts the cases of all persons arrested without warrant...

sections.section_title: Discharge of person arrested.
sections.content: Any person who has been arrested without a warrant by a peace officer shall not be discharged except on his own bond or on bail or under the special order in writing of a Magistrate.

sections.section_title: Offence committed in the presence of Magistrate.
sections.content: When any offence is committed in the presence of a Magistrate within the local limits of his jurisdiction he may himself arrest or order any person to arrest the offender and may thereupon, subject to the provisions herein contained as to bail, commit the offender to custody.

sections.section_title: Arrest by or in presence of Magistrate.
sections.content: Any Magistrate may at any time arrest or direct the arrest in his presence within the local limits of his jurisdiction of any person for whose arrest he is competent at the time and in the circumstances to issue a warrant.

sections.section_title: Recapture of person escaping.
sections.content: If a person in lawful custody escapes or is rescued the person from whose custody he escaped or was rescued may immediately pursue and arrest him in any place... and deal with such person as he might have done on the original taking.

sections.section_title: Provisions of sections 24,25 and 27 to apply to arrested under section 42.
sections.content: The sections 24, 25 and 27 shall apply to arrest under section 42 although the person making the arrest is not acting under a warrant and is not a peace officer having authority to arrest.

sections.section_title: Special provisions regarding persons arrested in respect of child abuse.
sections.content: (1) Sections 36 and 37 (24-hour rule) do not apply. (2) Person must be produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours. (3) Magistrate may, upon a certificate from a senior police officer, permit detention in police custody for up to three days for investigation. (4) After investigation or three days, person shall be produced before Magistrate and normal procedures apply.

sections.section_title: Duty of Magistrates to visit police stations
sections.content: (1) Every Magistrate must visit every police station in their division at least once a month to ensure suspects are protected as per the Convention Against Torture Act. (2) The Magistrate must personally see the suspect, check their well-being, and record observations and complaints. (3) If torture is suspected, the Magistrate can order a medical examination. (4) If the report confirms torture, the Magistrate shall make appropriate orders (medical treatment, change of custody). (5) The Magistrate shall direct the IGP to investigate the alleged torture.

subchapters.subchapter_title: A. - SUMMONS

sections.section_title: Registries of summons.
sections.content: (1) Every summons issued by a court... shall be in writing in duplicate and signed... and shall be in the prescribed form. (2) If the person summoned is believed not able to read the court's language, a translation shall be annexed. (3) A copy of the complaint and list of prosecution witnesses shall be attached.

sections.section_title: Summons how served.
sections.content: (1) Ordinarily served by the Fiscal; can be served by police, grama seva niladhari, or registered post in specific circumstances. (2) Shall if practicable be served personally. (3)-(5) Details service on companies, corporations, and associations.

sections.section_title: Service when person summoned cannot be found.
sections.content: When the person to be summoned cannot... be found the summons may be served by leaving one of the duplicates... with some adult member of his family or with his servant residing with him.

sections.section_title: Procedure when personal service cannot be effected.
sections.content: If service under sections 45 and 46 cannot be effected, the serving officer shall affix one of the duplicates... to some conspicuous part of the house or homestead... and... the summons... shall be deemed to have been duly served.

sections.section_title: Service on employees of the State, local authorities or corporations.
sections.content: Specifies the procedure for serving summons on government employees, military personnel, and employees of local authorities/corporations by sending the summons to the head of their department/office.

sections.section_title: Proof of service.
sections.content: Describes the admissible forms of proof for service of a summons, including affidavit, police officer's report, postal advice, or departmental endorsement.

subchapters.subchapter_title: B - WARRANT OF ARREST

sections.section_title: Form of warrant of arrest. Continuance of warrant of arrest.
sections.content: (1) Every warrant of arrest... shall be in writing, signed by a Judge... and shall be in the prescribed form. (2) Every such warrant shall remain in force until it is cancelled by the court which issued it or until it is executed.

sections.section_title: Court may direct security to be taken.
sections.content: Allows a court to endorse a warrant with a direction that if the person executes a bond with sureties for their attendance, the arresting officer shall take such security and release the person.

sections.section_title: Warrants to whom directed.
sections.content: Specifies that warrants are ordinarily directed to the Fiscal, but can be directed to other persons or police officers. Details rules on execution by named officers.

sections.section_title: Notification of substance of warrant.
sections.content: The person executing a warrant of arrest shall notify the substance thereof to the person arrested, and if so required... shall show him the warrant...

sections.section_title: Person arrested to be brought before court without delay.
sections.content: The person executing a warrant of arrest shall... without unnecessary delay bring the person arrested before the court... and he shall endorse on the warrant the time when and the place where the arrest was made.

sections.section_title: Where warrant may be executed.
sections.content: A warrant of arrest may be executed at any place in Sri Lanka.

sections.section_title: Warrants for execution outside jurisdiction.
sections.content: Details the procedure for executing warrants outside the issuing court's jurisdiction, including forwarding to the local Magistrate or special direction to a person for execution without endorsement in urgent cases.

sections.section_title: Warrant directed to execution outside jurisdiction.
sections.content: When a warrant directed to a Fiscal is to be executed outside the jurisdiction, the court shall ordinarily forward it to the Fiscal of the court within whose local limits the warrant is to be executed.

sections.section_title: Procedure on arrest of person against whom warrant is issued.
sections.content: Specifies the procedure when a person is arrested on a warrant outside the jurisdiction of the issuing court, including bringing them before the local Magistrate's Court and provisions for bail.

sections.section_title: Arrest possible though warrant is not in hand.
sections.content: Where a police officer has reasonable grounds to believe that a person is one for whose arrest a warrant of arrest has been issued, he may... arrest that person... although the warrant is not in his possession...

subchapters.subchapter_title: C - PROCLAMATION AND ATTACHMENT

sections.section_title: Proclamation for person absconding.
sections.content: If a court believes a person against whom a warrant has been issued has absconded, it may publish a written proclamation requiring him to appear within not less than 30 days. Details the method of publication.

sections.section_title: Attachment of property of person absconding.
sections.content: The court may, after issuing a proclamation, order the attachment of the proclaimed person's property. Details methods for attaching movable and immovable property, the role of a receiver, disposal of attached property, and registration of orders for immovable property.

sections.section_title: Restoration of attached property.
sections.content: Allows for the restoration of attached property (or sale proceeds) within one year if the person proves they did not abscond to avoid arrest and had no proper notice of the proclamation.

subchapters.subchapter_title: D- OTHER RULES REGARDING PROCESSES

sections.section_title: Issue of warrant in lieu of or in addition to summons. Court may require deposit to meet expenses of executing warrant.
sections.content: (1) A court may issue a warrant instead of a summons if it believes the person has absconded, will not obey the summons, or fails to appear after due service without reasonable excuse. (2) The court may require a deposit to cover the expenses of executing the warrant.

sections.section_title: Power to take bond for appearance.
sections.content: (1) When a person is present in court, the presiding officer may require them to execute a bond for their appearance. (2) If a person bound by such a bond fails to appear, a warrant for their arrest may be issued.

sections.section_title: Summons to run in any part of Sri Lanka.
sections.content: (1) Summons can be served anywhere in Sri Lanka if endorsed 'For service out of the jurisdiction'. (2) Such endorsement can only be made if the court is satisfied there are grounds. (3) Provisions for warrants apply, as far as possible, to summonses.

chapters.chapter_title: OF PROCESS TO COMPEL THE PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS AND OTHER MOVABLE PROPERTY AND FOR THE DISCOVERY OF PERSONS WRONGFULLY CONFINED

subchapters.subchapter_title: A. - SUMMONS TO PRODUCE

sections.section_title: Summons to produce document or other thing.
sections.content: (1) A court may issue a summons to a person to produce a document or thing necessary for any proceeding. (2) The person can cause the item to be produced without attending personally. (3) Does not affect certain sections of the Evidence Ordinance.

sections.section_title: Procedure as to letters, telegrams, & c.
sections.content: A court, the Attorney-General, or a senior police officer can require Postal/Telecommunication authorities to deliver documents needed for an investigation or proceeding.

subchapters.subchapter_title: B - SEARCH WARRANTS

sections.section_title: When search warrant may be issued.
sections.content: A court may issue a search warrant if: (a) a summons under S.66/requisition under S.67 will not be complied with; (b) the location of the document/thing is unknown; (c) a general search is needed. Warrants for postal/telecommunication documents require the High Court.

sections.section_title: Power to restrict warrant.
sections.content: The court may specify in the warrant the particular place or part to which the search shall be restricted.

sections.section_title: Search of house suspected to contain stolen property, forged documents, &c.
sections.content: A Magistrate may issue a warrant to search a place suspected of being used for stolen goods, forged documents/counterfeiting materials, and to seize such items and arrest persons found therein privy to the activities.

sections.section_title: Disposal of things found in search beyond jurisdiction.
sections.content: Specifies the procedure for handling items found during a search executed outside the jurisdiction of the issuing court.

subchapters.subchapter_title: C - DISCOVERY OF PERSONS WRONGFULLY CONFINED

sections.section_title: Search for person wrongfully confined.
sections.content: A Magistrate may issue a search warrant to search for a person wrongfully confined. The person, if found, shall be taken before the Magistrate.

subchapters.subchapter_title: D. - GENERAL PROVISION RELATING TO SEARCHES

sections.section_title: Direction, & c., of search warrants.
sections.content: The provisions regarding warrants of arrest (S.20, 50, 52, 56, 57) apply, as far as may be, to search warrants.

sections.section_title: Persons in charge of closed place to allow search.
sections.content: A person in charge of a place liable to search must allow free ingress and facilities for search. If ingress cannot be obtained, S.25 (breaking open doors) applies.

sections.section_title: Officer to make list of things seized.
sections.content: The person executing a search warrant shall make a list of all things seized and the places where they were found, and sign the list.

sections.section_title: Occupant of place searched may attend.
sections.content: The occupant of the place searched or their representative shall be permitted to attend during the search and shall receive a signed copy of the list of seized items.

subchapters.subchapter_title: E. - MISCELLANEOUS

sections.section_title: Court may impound things produced.
sections.content: Any court may if it thinks fit impound any document or other thing produced before it under this Code.

sections.section_title: Search warrants may be endorsed by peace officer.
sections.content: A search warrant directed to a peace officer may be executed by another peace officer if the original officer is unable to proceed, with an endorsement of the new officer's name.

sections.section_title: Powers of Magistrate when present at search.
sections.content: (1) The issuing Magistrate may attend personally to see the warrant duly executed. (2) Any Magistrate may orally direct a search in their presence of a place they are competent to issue a warrant for.

chapters.chapter_title: OF SECURITY FOR KEEPING THE PEACE /AND FOR GOOD BEHAVIOUR

subchapters.subchapter_title: A. - SECURITY FOR KEEPING THE PEACE ON CONVICTION

sections.section_title: Security for Keeping the peace on conviction.
sections.content: When a person is convicted of an offence involving a breach of the peace, criminal intimidation, or being a member of an unlawful assembly, the court may order them to execute a bond for keeping the peace for a period not exceeding 2 years (Magistrate's Court) or 3 years (High Court).

subchapters.subchapter_title: B. - SECURITY FOR KEEPING THE PEACE IN OTHER CASES AND SECURITY FOR GOOD BEHAVIOUR

sections.section_title: Security for keeping the peace in other cases
sections.content: A Magistrate may require a person to show cause why they should not execute a bond for keeping the peace if informed the person is likely to commit a breach of the peace.

sections.section_title: Security for behaviour from suspected persons, vagrants, & c
sections.content: A Magistrate may require a person to show cause why they should not execute a bond for good behaviour if the person is taking precautions to conceal their presence with a view to committing an offence, or has no ostensible means of subsistence/cannot give a satisfactory account of themselves.

sections.section_title: Security for good behaviour from habitual offenders.
sections.content: A Magistrate may require a person to show cause why they should not execute a bond for good behaviour if the person is a habitual robber, thief, housebreaker, receiver of stolen property, extortionist, protector of thieves, notorious bad liver, or dangerous character.

sections.section_title: Summons or warrant in case of person not so present.
sections.content: Procedure for issuing a summons or warrant to compel the appearance of a person against whom proceedings are initiated under S.81, 82, or 83. Allows for immediate arrest by warrant if necessary to prevent a breach of the peace.

sections.section_title: Form of summons or warrant.
sections.content: Every summons or warrant issued under section 84 shall contain a brief statement of the substance of the information on which it is issued.

sections.section_title: Inquiry as to the truth of information.
sections.content: Details the procedure for the inquiry into the information, including taking evidence. Evidence of general repute is admissible for proving habitual offending. The Magistrate can order an interim bond pending the inquiry.

sections.section_title: Order to give security.
sections.content: If the inquiry proves it is necessary for keeping the peace or maintaining good behaviour, the Magistrate shall order the person to execute a bond with or without sureties. The amount must not be excessive. For a minor, only the sureties execute the bond.

sections.section_title: Discharge of person informed against.
sections.content: If it is not proved that security is necessary, the Magistrate shall make an entry to that effect and release or discharge the person.

subchapters.subchapter_title: C - PROVISIONS APPLICABLE TO ALL ORDERS TO FURNISH SECURITY

sections.section_title: Imposition of term of imprisonment in default of security. Allowance of time to give security.
sections.content: The court shall direct imprisonment in default of giving security (max 2 years for HC, 1 year for MC). The court may allow up to one month to furnish security, unless the person is already undergoing imprisonment.

sections.section_title: Commencement of period for which security is required.
sections.content: Specifies when the period for which security is required commences, depending on whether time was allowed and if the person is already imprisoned.

sections.section_title: Contents of bond. Power to reject sureties.
sections.content: (1) The bond binds the person to keep the peace/be of good behaviour. Commission of an offence punishable with imprisonment is a breach. (2) A court may refuse a surety deemed unfit, recording reasons.

sections.section_title: Commitment to prison for default in giving security. Simple imprisonment for failure to give security for the peace. Rigorous or simple imprisonment for failure to give security for good behaviour.
sections.content: (1) If security is not given by the commencement date, the person shall be committed to prison. (2) Imprisonment for failure to give security for keeping the peace shall be Simple. (3) Imprisonment for failure to give security for good behaviour may be rigorous or simple.

sections.section_title: Power to release person imprisoned for failing to give security.
sections.content: A court may order the discharge of a person imprisoned for failing to give security if it believes they can be released without hazard. Lower courts can only do this for their own orders.

subchapters.subchapter_title: D. - DISCHARGE OF SURETIES

sections.section_title: Discharge of sureties.
sections.content: A surety can apply to the court to cancel the bond. The court can then summon the principal and order them to provide fresh security for the unexpired term.

sections.section_title: Dispersal of unlawful assembly. Use of civil force to disperse assembly. Use of military force to disperse assembly.
sections.content: (1) A Magistrate or police officer (Inspector or above) may command an unlawful assembly or assembly likely to cause disturbance to disperse. (2) If it does not disperse, they may use necessary force and require public assistance. (3) If it cannot be otherwise dispersed, a Magistrate, Government Agent, or senior police officer can require the military to disperse it.

sections.section_title: Power of commissioned military officers to disperse assembly.
sections.content: A commissioned military officer may disperse an assembly by military force if public security is manifestly endangered and civil authorities cannot be communicated with. Must obey instructions if communication becomes possible.

sections.section_title: Protection for acts done under this Chapter.
sections.content: (1) No prosecution without the sanction of the Attorney-General. (2) Provides immunity from civil or criminal liability for acts done in good faith under this Chapter, and for military personnel obeying lawful orders.

subchapters.subchapter_title: A. - ORDERS FOR REMOVAL OR ABATEMENT IN CASES OF NUISANCE

sections.section_title: Conditional order for removal of nuisance.
sections.content: A Magistrate may make a conditional order for the removal of various types of nuisances, including unlawful obstructions, trades injurious to health, dangerous buildings/trees, and unfenced excavations.

sections.section_title: Service or notification of order.
sections.content: The order shall be served like a summons, or posted up if service is not practicable.

sections.section_title: Person to whom order is addressed to obey or show cause. Consequence of failing to do so.
sections.content: The person must perform the act or appear to have the order set aside/modified. Failure to do so makes them liable to a penalty and the order becomes absolute.

sections.section_title: Procedure in case of appearance.
sections.content: If the person appears, the Magistrate takes evidence. The order is then rescinded, modified, or made absolute based on the findings.

sections.section_title: Procedure on order being made absolute.
sections.content: After an order is made absolute, the Magistrate shall give notice to the person, requiring performance of the act and warning of penalties for disobedience.

sections.section_title: Consequence of disobedience to order.
sections.content: If the act is not performed, the Magistrate may cause it to be performed and recover the costs by sale of property.

sections.section_title: Injunction pending inquiry.
sections.content: The Magistrate may issue an immediate injunction to prevent imminent danger or serious injury to the public.

sections.section_title: Magistrate may prohibit continuance of public nuisances.
sections.content: A Magistrate may order any person not to repeat or continue a public nuisance.

subchapters.subchapter_title: B. - TEMPORARY ORDERS IN URGENT CASES OF NUISANCE

sections.section_title: Power to issue absolute order at once in urgent cases of nuisance.
sections.content: In urgent cases, a Magistrate may issue an immediate, absolute order to prevent obstruction, annoyance, injury, danger to life, or a riot/affray. Can be made ex parte in emergencies. Lasts for a maximum of 14 days unless extended by the Minister.

sections.section_title: Peace officers to prevent cognizable offence. Information of attempt to commit such offence. Peace officers may arrest without order or warrant to prevent such offence. Prevention of injury to public property. Assistance to inspectors of weights and measures.
sections.content: (1) Every peace officer shall interpose to prevent cognizable offences. (2) Shall communicate information of attempts to commit such offences. (3) May arrest without warrant to prevent such offences if necessary. (4) May prevent injury to public property. (5) Duty to assist inspectors of weights and measures.

chapters.chapter_title: INFORMATION TO POLICE OFFICERS AND INQUIRERS AND THEIR POWERS TO INVESTIGATE

sections.section_title: Appointment of inquirers.
sections.content: The Minister may appoint any person by name or office to be an inquirer for any specified area.

sections.section_title: Information of an offence.
sections.content: Details the procedure for giving information about an offence to a police officer or inquirer: must be in writing or reduced to writing, signed by the informant, and entered in the Information Book. Specifies the duties of the officer/inquirer upon receiving information, including when to investigate and when not to. Grants power to require the attendance of persons acquainted with the case.

sections.section_title: Examination of witnesses by police officer or inquirer.
sections.content: (1) Details the procedure for recording statements from witnesses during investigation: no oath, full recording in a specific manner, reading back/interpretation, liberty to explain/add, signing, and a certificate by the officer. (2) The person is bound to answer all questions except those tending to incriminate themselves. (3) Specifies the limited use of statements made to police (not for corroboration, but for contradiction or under S.27 of Evidence Ordinance). (4) Courts may send for statements recorded in an investigation and may use such statements or information, not as evidence in the case, but to aid it in such inquiry or trial. Save as otherwise provided for in section 444 neither the accused nor his agents shall be entitled to call for such statements, nor shall he or they be entitled to see them merely because they are referred to by the court but if they are used by the police officer or inquirer or witness who made them to refresh his memory, or if the court uses them for the purpose of contradicting such police officer or inquirer or witness the provisions of the Evidence Ordinance, section 161 or section 145, as the case may be, shall apply: Provided that where a preliminary inquiry under Chapter XV is being held in respect of any offence, such statements of witnesses as have up to then been recorded shall, on the application of the accused, be made available to him for his perusal in open court during the inquiry.

sections.section_title: Inducement not to be offered.
sections.content: Any inquirer or police officer shall not offer or make or cause to be offered or made any inducement, threat, or promise to any person charged with an offence to induce such person to make any statement with reference to the charge against such person. But any inquirer or police officer shall not prevent or discourage by any caution or otherwise any person from making in the course of any investigation under this Chapter any statement which he may be disposed to make of his own free will.

sections.section_title: Search by police officers or inquirer.
sections.content: (1) If an officer or inquirer considers production of a document/thing necessary for investigation and believes a summons under S.66 will not be complied with, or the item's location is unknown, they may search for it. (2) The officer/inquirer shall practicable conduct the search in person. (3) If unable, they may require a Grama Seva Niladhari to conduct the search with a written order. (4) Provisions for search warrants apply to such searches.

sections.section_title: Duty of subordinate officer to report to officer in charge of station.
sections.content: When any subordinate police officer has made any investigation under this Chapter, he shall report the result of such investigation to the officer in charge of the police station.

sections.section_title: Release of accused if evidence deficient.
sections.content: If upon an investigation it appears to the officer in charge of the police station or the inquirer that there is not sufficient evidence or reasonable ground of suspicion to justify forwarding the accused to a Magistrate's Court, such officer or inquirer shall, if the person is in custody, release him on his executing a bond with or without sureties to appear if and when required.

sections.section_title: Procedure when investigation cannot be completed in twenty-four hours.
sections.content: (1) If an investigation cannot be completed within 24 hours and further investigation is necessary, the officer/inquirer shall forward the suspect to the Magistrate and transmit a report and witness summary. (2) The Magistrate may, if satisfied, authorize detention for a total period of fifteen days. (3) Places restrictions on bail for persons accused of offences under specific sections of the Penal Code. (4) Allows court-authorized police access to the suspect in prison for investigation purposes.

sections.section_title: Duty of officer or inquirer to forward case to a Magistrate's Court if sufficient information is well founded.
sections.content: (1) If upon investigation it appears the information is well founded, the officer/inquirer shall forward the suspect to the Magistrate's Court or take a bond for appearance if the offence is bailable. (2) They shall send relevant articles to court and require complainant/witnesses to execute bonds to appear. (3) The Magistrate may forward items to experts for analysis. (4) Bonds executed shall be sent to court. (5) If a complainant/witness refuses to execute a bond, the Magistrate may issue a warrant/summons.

sections.section_title: Additional powers of inquirers.
sections.content: Every inquirer shall, within the local limits of his jurisdiction, have the power to arrest or direct the arrest of any offender in his presence and the power to issue a warrant or order the removal of an accused person arrested under a warrant.

sections.section_title: Power to investigate non-cognizable offence as if cognizable.
sections.content: (1) If an inquirer or police officer investigating a non-cognizable offence opines it necessary to exercise powers applicable only to cognizable offences, they may do so upon receiving an order from a Magistrate. (2) Subject to S.37, every inquirer and OIC has the power to authorize detention during an investigation.

sections.section_title: Magistrate may withdraw case from inquirer.
sections.content: Any Magistrate having jurisdiction may at any stage withdraw a case from an inquirer and himself inquire into and try the case or commit it for trial.

sections.section_title: Investigation to be completed without delay.
sections.content: (1) Every investigation shall be completed without unnecessary delay. If not completed within 15 days, a progress report must be sent to the Magistrate every 15 days. (2) Detention ordered under S.115(3) is for periods of 15 days at a time. (3) Upon completion, a final report in prescribed form must be forwarded. The Magistrate shall discharge the suspect if no offence is alleged, or prosecute if one is alleged.

sections.section_title: Saving of powers of police officers.
sections.content: Anything in this Chapter shall not be construed to restrict the powers or duties vested in or imposed on police officers by this Code or any other enactment.

sections.section_title: Medical examination of person by medical practitioner.
sections.content: (1) An OIC can, with consent, cause a person to be examined by a Government Medical Officer for the investigation. (2) If consent is refused, the OIC can apply to a Magistrate for an order authorizing the examination.

sections.section_title: Medical examination in case of an offence alleged to have been committed by a child of, or above, twelve years of age and under fourteen.
sections.content: (1) The OIC investigating an offence by a child (12-14 years) shall, with parental consent, have the child examined by a multidisciplinary team to assess maturity and responsibility. (2) The team comprises a judicial medical officer, a pediatric/adolescent psychiatrist, and a psychologist. (3) If consent is refused, the OIC shall apply to the Magistrate for an order. (4)-(6) Details procedures for obtaining the report, subjecting the child to rehabilitation, and submitting the report to the Magistrate.

sections.section_title: Taking of finger impressions, specimens of hair & c. of suspect person.
sections.content: (1) An OIC may, with consent, take bodily impressions, specimens (blood, saliva, etc.), or scrapings from a suspect. (2) If consent is refused, the OIC may apply to the Magistrate for an order. (3) An OIC may, with consent, take a specimen of handwriting. (4) If refusal, the OIC may apply to the Magistrate for an order.

sections.section_title: Magistrate to assist investigation.
sections.content: Every Magistrate shall assist an investigation by making appropriate orders and processes, and may in particular hold or authorize an identification parade.

sections.section_title: Senior police officer may take over investigation.
sections.content: Notwithstanding any other law, any police officer not below the rank of Assistant Superintendent of Police may take over and conduct any investigation at any stage.

chapters.chapter_title: STATEMENTS TO MAGISTRATES OR PEACE OFFICERS

sections.section_title: No inducement to be offered.
sections.content: Except as provided in Chapter XXI, no peace officer or person in authority shall offer any inducement, threat, or promise to a person charged with an offence to make a statement. But they shall not prevent or discourage a person from making a statement of their own free will.

sections.section_title: Notice of alibi.
sections.content: (1) A person is not entitled to adduce evidence of an alibi during a High Court trial unless they have stated it to the police during investigation, during the preliminary inquiry, or given notice to the AG at least 14 days before trial (with court discretion to allow later). (2) The original statement must contain details of time, place, and supporting witnesses. (3) Defines 'evidence in support of an alibi'.

sections.section_title: Power to record statements and confessions.
sections.content: (1) Any Magistrate may record any statement made to him before the commencement of any inquiry or trial. (2) The statement shall be recorded and signed as per S.277 and forwarded to the trial court. (3) A confession shall not be recorded unless the Magistrate believes it was made voluntarily, and a memorandum to that effect must be appended.

chapters.chapter_title: OF THE JURISDICTION OF THE CRIMINAL COURTS IN INQUIRIES AND TRIALS

subchapters.subchapter_title: A - PLACE OF INQUIRY OR TRIAL

sections.section_title: Ordinary place of inquiry and trial. Any Magistrate's Court to have jurisdiction over offences committed on territorial waters.
sections.content: (1) Every offence shall ordinarily be inquired into and tried by a court within the local limits of whose jurisdiction it was committed. (2) Any Magistrate's Court within whose jurisdiction an accused is found has jurisdiction over offences committed on territorial waters. (3) Offences on territorial waters (where S.128(2) doesn't apply), high seas, ships, or aircraft may be tried by the Colombo Magistrate's Court or the High Court.

sections.section_title: Accused is triable in district where act is done or consequence ensues.
sections.content: When an offence is committed by an act and a consequence, it may be tried by any court within whose jurisdiction any such thing was done or any such consequence ensued. Provides illustrations.

sections.section_title: Place of trial where act is an offence by reason of relation to other offence.
sections.content: A charge of an offence which exists by relation to another offence may be inquired into or tried by a court within whose jurisdiction either act was done. Provides illustrations.

sections.section_title: Escape from custody. Criminal misappropriation and criminal breach of trust. Stealing.
sections.content: (1) Escape from custody may be tried where the accused is found or where the escape occurred. (2) Criminal misappropriation/breach of trust may be tried where any part of the property was received by the accused or where the offence was committed. (3) Stealing may be tried where the thing was stolen, possessed by the thief, or received.

sections.section_title: Place of inquiry or trial in various cases. Offences committed on a journey. Offences against provisions of any law relating to railways, telecommunications, post office, arms and ammunition.
sections.content: (1) Specifies various scenarios (uncertain location, offence partly in multiple areas, continuing offence, several acts in different areas, same transaction different offences in different areas) where an offence may be tried by a court having jurisdiction over any one of the local areas. (2) An offence committed during a journey/voyage may be tried by a court through whose jurisdiction the offender/victim/thing passed. (3) Offences against laws relating to railways, telecom, post office, or arms/ammunition may be tried by any court where the offender is found.

sections.section_title: Attorney-General to decide, in case of doubt court where inquiry shall take place.
sections.content: If a Magistrate is in doubt about which court should inquire into an offence, they may state a case to the Attorney-General, who shall decide.

sections.section_title: Sentence not to be set aside because inquiry held by wrong Magistrate's Court.
sections.content: A sentence or order shall not be set aside merely because the inquiry was made by a Magistrate's Court not empowered under this Chapter to do so.

subchapters.subchapter_title: B. - CONDITIONS NECESSARY FOR INITIATING PROCEEDINGS

sections.section_title: The conditions necessary for the initiation of prosecutions for certain offences.
sections.content: (1) Specifies offences (e.g., under specific sections of the Penal Code) for which a court cannot take cognizance without the previous sanction of the Attorney-General or on the complaint of the concerned public servant or court. (2) The complaint of a court shall be in writing under the hand of the Registrar. (3) The court may cause the accused to be arrested. (4) Sanction remains in force for six months. (5) 'Some other person' includes a peace officer. (6) This section does not apply if the AG has exercised powers under S.393(7).

chapters.chapter_title: OF THE COMMENCEMENT OF PROCEEDINGS BEFORE MAGISTRATES COURTS

sections.section_title: Proceedings in Magistrate's Court how instituted.
sections.content: (1) Proceedings are instituted: (a) on a complaint (oral/written, written to be drawn by pleader); (b) on a written report by an inquirer, peace officer, public servant, etc.; (c) upon the knowledge/suspicion of the Magistrate (accused can require trial by another Magistrate); (d) on a person being brought in custody without process; (e) upon a warrant from the AG; (f) on a written complaint by a court under S.135. (2) Reports, AG's warrant, court complaints may be sent by post/messenger/hand. (3) Except as provided, no written complaint shall be entertained.

sections.section_title: Medical examination complainant and accused in case of rape, &c.
sections.content: In cases of rape, carnal intercourse with a young girl, unnatural offence, or serious hurt (especially by stabbing/cutting instrument), the Magistrate shall cause the alleged victim and the accused to be examined by a Government Medical Officer if not already done.

sections.section_title: Procedure in certain cases where accused is unknown.
sections.content: (1) If the accused is unknown and the offence is not summarily triable, the Magistrate may examine the complainant/informant and others on oath (can be in private). (2) The examination shall be recorded, read over, interpreted if needed, signed, and dated. (3) If sufficient ground exists, the Magistrate shall issue process.

sections.section_title: Issue of process.
sections.content: (1) Where proceedings are instituted and the Magistrate opines sufficient ground exists against a person not in custody: (a) if the First Schedule indicates summons, issue summons; (b) if it indicates warrant, issue warrant. Provides provisos for issuing summons instead of warrant, examining complainant/witness before warrant/summons, and recording facts in case of Magistrate's knowledge/suspicion. (2) & (3) Procedure for issuing process when proceedings instituted under other paragraphs of S.136(1).

sections.section_title: Contents of summons or warrant.
sections.content: Every summons or warrant shall state the particulars of the offence. A summons shall require the accused to appear at a specified time/place.

sections.section_title: Examination under section 139 to be recorded.
sections.content: Every examination held by the Magistrate under S.139 shall be recorded as per S.138(2) and may be held in private.

sections.section_title: Procedure to be adopted when case proceeds.
sections.content: (1) If the offence is in the Second Schedule to the Judicature Act or the AG directs, follow Chapter XV (Preliminary Inquiry). (2) If the offence is summarily triable, follow Chapter XVII (Summary Trial), unless the Magistrate thinks it cannot be adequately punished, in which case forward to AG.

sections.section_title: In case of homicide Magistrate to hold inquiry on spot.
sections.content: In a case of culpable homicide, the Magistrate shall, unless inexpedient, go to the spot and hold part of the inquiry or examine persons able to give material evidence. The examination shall be recorded.

sections.section_title: When issuing summons personal attendance of the accused may be dispensed with.
sections.content: When issuing a summons, the Magistrate may dispense with the accused's personal attendance and permit appearance by a pleader, but may later enforce personal attendance.

chapters.chapter_title: OF THE INQUIRY INTO CASES WHICH APPEAR NOT TO BE TRIABLE SUMMARILY BY MAGISTRATE'S COURT BUT TRIABLE BY THE HIGH COURT

sections.section_title: Preliminary inquiry.
sections.content: A Magistrate shall hold a preliminary inquiry in a case where: (a) the offence is in the Second Schedule to the Judicature Act; or (b) the AG, within 3 months of the offence, directs that evidence recorded at a preliminary inquiry is necessary.

sections.section_title: Charge to be read over to accused.
sections.content: The Magistrate shall read the charge(s) to the accused at the commencement. The accused is not required to reply, and any reply shall not be recorded or used in evidence.

sections.section_title: Certified copies of notes of investigation to be furnished to Magistrate.
sections.content: The OIC shall furnish the Magistrate with three certified copies of the notes of investigation and all statements recorded.

sections.section_title: Evidence for prosecution to be taken. Depositions where the accused is absconding or abroad.
sections.content: (1) The Magistrate shall take the statements on oath of those who know the facts (depositions). Expert witness evidence is usually by report only, unless the AG directs otherwise. (2) The accused may cross-examine prosecution witnesses. (3) If unrepresented, the Magistrate shall ask the accused if he wishes to question each witness. (4) Allows the inquiry to proceed in the absence of the accused if they are absconding, ill (with consent/no prejudice), or obstructing proceedings. An attorney-at-law may appear for an absent accused.

sections.section_title: Variance between charge and evidence.
sections.content: (1) Variance in time/place is not material if the charge was laid within the time limit and jurisdiction is not ousted. (2) Variance in other respects is not material, but the accused cannot be convicted of a different offence unless it is one for which he could be lawfully convicted on the original charge. (3) If the variance deceives/misleads the accused, the Magistrate may adjourn. (4) The Magistrate may amend the charge and recall witnesses.

sections.section_title: Charge to be read to accused after close prosecution case.
sections.content: After the prosecution case and if the Magistrate does not discharge under S.153, the charge shall be read and explained to the accused, and he shall be informed of his right to call witnesses and give evidence.

sections.section_title: Provisions as to taking statement of accused.
sections.content: (1) The Magistrate shall ask the accused if he wishes to say anything in answer to the charge, cautioning that he is not obliged but anything he says will be recorded and used in evidence. (2) The Magistrate shall also caution that no promise or threat should induce a confession. (3) The statement shall be recorded as per S.277.

sections.section_title: Evidence for the defence.
sections.content: (1) After the accused's statement (or if none), the Magistrate shall ask if he desires to give evidence and call witnesses. (2)-(5) Details the procedure for taking defence evidence, including adjournments for absent witnesses and binding over witnesses (except character witnesses).

sections.section_title: Discharge.
sections.content: (1) If the evidence is insufficient, the Magistrate shall discharge the accused for that charge (but this does not bar a subsequent charge or investigating other charges). (2) The Magistrate may discharge the accused at any stage if the complaint is groundless.

sections.section_title: Commitment for trial.
sections.content: (1) If the evidence is sufficient, the Magistrate shall commit the accused for trial before the High Court. (2) The accused may state willingness to plead guilty to a lesser offence, which shall be recorded. (3) & (4) The High Court, when sentencing after a guilty plea to a lesser offence, shall have regard to the accused's earlier indication, but there is no obligation to accept the plea.

sections.section_title: Accused's witnesses.
sections.content: (1) Upon committal, the accused shall state the names of witnesses he wishes to be required at trial. (2) The Magistrate shall prepare a list (excluding witnesses deemed immaterial) and direct the Fiscal to issue notices. (3) A notice has the same effect as a summons. (4) Service of the notice is as per summons provisions.

sections.section_title: Material witnesses to be bound over to appear.
sections.content: (1) The Magistrate shall require every material witness (prosecution/defence) who has given evidence to execute a bond to appear at the trial. (2) The Magistrate may require a bond for appearance at further proceedings. (3) If a witness refuses, the Magistrate may commit them to prison. (4) Names of bound-over or committed witnesses shall be endorsed on the warrant. (5) Witnesses shall give an address for notices.

sections.section_title: Magistrate to certify record.
sections.content: The Magistrate shall record whether the accused is on bail or in custody and certify the record of the inquiry.

sections.section_title: Accused entitled to statements to the police.
sections.content: (1) A committed accused is entitled to a certified copy of the police statements of witnesses who testified and of his own statement, on payment. (2) The AG may, for offences not triable by a Magistrate's Court, make available to the accused the statement of any witness not listed in the indictment.

sections.section_title: Record to be forwarded to court of trial, and certified copy of record to Attorney-General.
sections.content: (1) The Magistrate shall commit the accused to prison until trial. (2) The Magistrate shall forthwith transmit the record, certified copies, and investigation notes to the High Court and the Attorney-General. (3) S.264 applies to the warrant.

sections.section_title: Presentation and service of indictment.
sections.content: (1) If the AG opines the case should be tried, an indictment shall be drawn, signed per S.162(3), and filed in the High Court. (2) Filing the indictment is equivalent to stating all legal conditions are fulfilled. (3) The AG may substitute or include any charge disclosed by the evidence.

sections.section_title: When trial shall be by jury and when not.
sections.content: Subject to other laws, all prosecutions on indictment shall be tried by a Judge of the High Court. Provided that if at least one offence is in the Second Schedule to the Judicature Act, trial shall be by a jury before a Judge if and only if the accused elects for a jury trial.

sections.section_title: Contents of indictment.
sections.content: (1) Every indictment shall contain a list of prosecution witnesses and a list of documents/things (productions). The prosecution can call other witnesses/productions after notice. (2) Specifies documents to be attached to the indictment (e.g., record of inquiry, police statements, reports, identification parade notes, accused's statement to Magistrate, scene observations). (3) The indictment is in prescribed form, brought in the AG's name, and signed by the AG, Solicitor-General, or a State Counsel. (4) Proceedings do not abate due to AG's death/removal.

sections.section_title: Inquiry to be concluded in a month.
sections.content: Every preliminary inquiry shall be concluded within one month unless the Magistrate, recording reasons, finds it necessary to prolong it.

sections.section_title: Charge to state offence.
sections.content: (1) Every charge shall state the offence. (2) If the law gives a specific name, use it. (3) If not, state enough of the definition to give notice. (4) Mention the law and section. (5) The charge is equivalent to a statement that every legal condition was fulfilled. (6) The charge shall be read to the accused in a language he understands. Provides illustrations.

sections.section_title: Particulars as to time, place and person.
sections.content: (1) The charge shall contain sufficient particulars of time, place, person, and thing to give the accused notice. (2) For criminal breach of trust/misappropriation, specifying a gross sum/quantity and dates within a one-year period is sufficient for one offence. (3) If the above particulars are insufficient, the charge shall also contain particulars of the manner of committing the offence. Provides illustrations. (4) Words in a charge are deemed used in the sense attached by the law.

sections.section_title: Effect of errors.
sections.content: Errors or omissions in the charge are not material unless the accused was misled by them. Provides illustrations.

sections.section_title: Court may alter charge.
sections.content: (1) Any court may alter any indictment or charge before judgment (or before jury verdict in HC). (2) The alteration shall be read and explained to the accused. (3) Substituting or adding a charge is deemed an alteration.

sections.section_title: When trial may proceed on altered charge immediately.
sections.content: If the alteration is not likely to prejudice the accused or prosecutor, the court may proceed immediately.

sections.section_title: When new trial may be directed or trial adjourned.
sections.content: If the alteration is likely to cause prejudice, the court may direct a new trial or adjourn the trial.

sections.section_title: Stay of proceedings if prosecution of offence in altered charge requires previous sanction.
sections.content: If the altered charge requires previous sanction, the case shall not proceed until sanction is obtained, unless sanction was already obtained for a prosecution on the same facts.

sections.section_title: Recall of witnesses when charge altered.
sections.content: When a charge is altered after the trial begins, the prosecutor and accused may recall and re-examine witnesses regarding the alteration.

sections.section_title: Effect of material error.
sections.content: (1) If the Supreme Court or Court of Appeal is of the opinion that a convicted person was misled by an error in the charge, it shall direct a new trial or make another order. (2) If no valid charge can be preferred, it shall quash the conviction. Provides an illustration.

sections.section_title: Separate charge of separate offence.
sections.content: For every distinct offence, there shall be a separate charge and separate trial, except in cases mentioned in S.174, 175, 176, and 180. Provides an illustration.

sections.section_title: Three offences of same kind within a year may be charged together.
sections.content: (1) A person accused of more than one offence of the same kind committed within 12 months may be charged and tried at one trial for any number not exceeding three. (2) Offences are of the same kind if punishable with the same amount of punishment under the same section.

sections.section_title: Trial for more than one offence. Offence falling within two or more definitions. Acts constituting one offence but constituting another offence when combined.
sections.content: (1) If in one series of acts forming the same transaction more offences than one are committed, the person may be charged and tried for every such offence. (2) If acts constitute an offence falling within two or more separate definitions, the person may be charged and tried for each. (3) If several acts constitute when combined a different offence, the person may be charged and tried for the combined offence and for any offence constituted by one or more of the acts. (4) Does not affect S.67 of the Penal Code. Provides illustrations.

sections.section_title: Where it is doubtful what offence has been committed.
sections.content: If it is doubtful which of several offences the facts constitute, the accused may be charged with all or any of them, or with having committed one of them without specifying which. Provides an illustration.

sections.section_title: When a person charged with one offence may be convicted of a different offence.
sections.content: If an accused is charged with one offence and evidence shows he committed a different offence for which he might have been charged under S.176, he may be convicted of that different offence. Provides an illustration.

sections.section_title: When offence proved included in offence charged.
sections.content: (1) If a person is charged with an offence and the evidence proves a minor offence included in it, he may be convicted of the minor offence. (2) This applies even if jurisdiction for the minor offence is vested in another court. (3) Does not authorize conviction for offences requiring a complaint under S.135 without such complaint. Provides illustrations.

sections.section_title: Conviction of an attempt to commit an offence though attempt is not separately charged.
sections.content: If a person is charged with an offence and the evidence proves an attempt, he may be convicted of the attempt, provided an attempt is punishable by law.

sections.section_title: All persons concerned in committing an offence may be charged together.
sections.content: Persons accused of jointly committing the same offence, different offences in the same transaction, or one of committing and another of abetment/attempt, may be charged and tried together or separately. Provides illustrations.

sections.section_title: When convicted on one charge remaining charges may be withdrawn.
sections.content: (1) When more than one charge is made and a conviction is had on one, the prosecution may withdraw remaining charges, or the court may stay proceedings. (2) Withdrawal is an acquittal unless the conviction is set aside, in which case the court may proceed.

chapters.chapter_title: THE TRIAL OF CASES WHERE A MAGISTRATE’S COURT HAS POWER TO TRY SUMMARILY

sections.section_title: Particulars of case to be stated to accused.
sections.content: (1) If there is sufficient ground, the Magistrate shall frame a charge. (2) The Magistrate shall read the charge and ask the accused if he has any cause to show why he should not be convicted.

sections.section_title: Plea of guilty and sentencing without written plea agreement.
sections.content: (1) If the accused makes an unqualified admission of guilt, it is recorded, a verdict of guilty is recorded, and sentence is passed. The accused may withdraw the plea before sentence. (2) If no admission or a qualified admission, the Magistrate asks if ready for trial and proceeds accordingly, with provisions for adjournment.

sections.section_title: Plea of guilty and sentencing with written plea agreement.
sections.content: (1) A plea agreement may be entered into between the prosecutor and accused before sentence. (2) The court shall be informed but not participate. (3) The prosecutor, accused's attorney, or accused may initiate, but the prosecutor has sole discretion. (4) For certain offences, the accused must be fingerprinted. (5)-(7) Details the negotiation process, the prosecutor's duties (considering circumstances, not using info if negotiations fail, allowing victim input). (8) Details on sentence recommendations, court's discretion, and consequences of parties deviating from the agreement. (9)-(10) Procedure for presenting the plea agreement and court acceptance. (11) Upon acceptance, the agreement is binding, and the accused is convicted. (12)-(13) Limits on appeals against conviction and sentence. (14)-(16) Procedure and consequences if the court rejects the agreement. (17) Defines 'child' and 'prosecutor'.

sections.section_title: Procedure on trial.
sections.content: (1) The Magistrate shall take all evidence produced by prosecution and defence. (2) The accused may cross-examine prosecution witnesses. (3) The complainant and accused may open their cases, but the complainant has no right of reply to defence evidence.

sections.section_title: Verdict.
sections.content: (1) If the Magistrate finds the accused not guilty, he records an acquittal; if guilty, he records a verdict of guilty and passes sentence. (2) The Magistrate may consider time spent in custody prior to conviction as part of the sentence, unless a mandatory minimum sentence is stipulated. (3) Defines 'custody' as remand custody.

sections.section_title: Power of Magistrate to discharge accused at any time.
sections.content: The Magistrate may discharge the accused at any previous stage, recording reasons. If satisfied further proceedings will not result in conviction, he shall acquit.

sections.section_title: What to be done when different offence disclosed in course of proceedings.
sections.content: (1) If a different offence within the Magistrate's jurisdiction is disclosed, he may convict after framing and explaining a new charge. (2) If an offence in the Second Schedule (High Court) is disclosed, he shall start proceedings under Chapter XV. If a non-summarily triable offence (not in Second Schedule) is disclosed, he shall stay proceedings and report to the AG.

sections.section_title: Accused may be acquitted in the absence of complainant.
sections.content: (1) If the complainant under S.136(1)(a) does not appear, the Magistrate shall acquit the accused unless he adjourns, and may order State costs. (2) If prosecution under S.136(1)(b) or (c) is not ready, the court may discharge the accused. (3) Discharge under (2) operates as an acquittal after one year or upon a second discharge, subject to pending applications.

sections.section_title: Withdrawal of charge by complainant.
sections.content: If a complainant satisfies the Magistrate of sufficient grounds to withdraw, the Magistrate may permit withdrawal and acquit the accused, recording reasons. Does not extend powers to compound offences.

sections.section_title: Accused may be discharged by Magistrate with sanction of Attorney-General.
sections.content: In cases not instituted on a complaint under S.136(1)(a), (c), or (d), the Magistrate may, with the AG's previous sanction, stop proceedings and discharge the accused without a judgment of acquittal or conviction.

sections.section_title: By whom prosecution under this chapter maybe conducted.
sections.content: (1) The AG, Solicitor-General, State Counsel, or an authorized pleader is entitled to conduct the prosecution. In their absence, the complainant or an interested officer/authority may prosecute. The Magistrate may permit any attorney-at-law to prosecute on behalf of the victim. (2) In complaints under S.136(1)(a), the AG etc. shall not appear for the complainant without his consent, except in cases against state officers related to official duties.

sections.section_title: Trial may proceed in absence of accused.
sections.content: (1) The Magistrate may proceed with the trial in the absence of the accused if they are absconding, ill (with consent/no prejudice), or obstructing proceedings. (2) If the accused appears bona fide during or soon after the trial: (a) if trial not concluded, evidence is read and cross-examination allowed; (b) if concluded, conviction/sentence is set aside and a new trial ordered. (3) This does not apply if the accused was represented by an attorney during absence.

subchapters.subchapter_title: A - GENERAL

sections.section_title: By whom trials before High Court to be conducted.
sections.content: In every trial before the High Court, the prosecution shall be conducted by the Attorney-General, Solicitor-General, a State Counsel, or a pleader authorized by the Attorney-General.

sections.section_title: Attorney-General may withdraw prosecution.
sections.content: (1) The Attorney-General may, before the verdict, inform the court that he will not further prosecute, and the accused shall be discharged. (2) The information may be oral or written. (3) Prosecuting counsel may, with the presiding Judge's consent, withdraw the indictment or any charge.

sections.section_title: Duty of Judge upon receipt of indictment.
sections.content: Upon receipt of the indictment, the Judge shall: (a) cause the accused to appear/before him; (b) serve a copy of the indictment with annexes on each accused; (c) inform the accused and aggrieved party of the pre-trial conference date; (d) direct the accused to execute a bond or commit to prison; (e) cause the accused to be fingerprinted; (f) if jury trial, direct the accused to elect a jury panel; (g) assign an attorney-at-law if requested.

subchapters.subchapter_title: A-1- PRE-TRIAL CONFERENCES

sections.section_title: Pre-trial conferences to be held at the High Court.
sections.content: (1) A pre-trial conference shall be held upon indictment being served on the accused in terms of section 195, in the presence of the accused; or where the accused is not present due to any reason specified in paragraph (a) of subsection (1) of section 241, or where it is not possible to serve the indictment on the accused due to the reasons specified in paragraph (b) of subsection (1) of section 241, in the absence of the accused. (2) A pre-trial conference shall be held with the participation of the prosecuting counsel; counsel, if any, appearing on behalf of the accused; the aggrieved party or the counsel, if any, appearing on behalf of the aggrieved party, on the application of such aggrieved party or such counsel to participate in the pre-trial conference; the officer in charge for the time being of the police station in which the investigation in respect of the offence has been conducted or an officer representing him; and an Attorney-at-Law or any other officer permitted by the Presiding Judge, with the consent of the parties to the case, to participate. (3) A pre-trial conference shall be held for the purposes of ascertaining whether the prosecution has handed over to the accused, all material, the accused is legally entitled to receive, and for the purpose of making appropriate orders, by Court in that regard; ascertaining whether the accused intends to plead guilty to any one or more of the charges in the indictment, or to a lesser offence; or whether he intends to negotiate for a plea agreement under section 197A; providing an opportunity to the accused to give advance notice of his mitigatory or exculpatory defence and that of an alibi as specified in section 126A; inquiring, from the accused whether or not he elects to be tried by a jury or from the prosecuting counsel or the accused whether a special jury referred to in section 208 is required, if the indictment relates to an offence triable by a jury; ascertaining whether reports of expert witnesses have been received by court and served on the accused, and if not making appropriate orders in that regard; recording admissions of the accused, if any, including the admissions relating to the reports of expert witnesses and the content of such reports, having regard to the provisions of section 420;
